column6: A continental dataset that includes annual shorelines and rates of coastal change along the entire African coastline. Generated for 2000 to 2023.  Combines satellite data from the Digital Earth Africa program with tidal modelling to map the typical location of the coastline at mean sea level each year.  Data used was  regularly spaced point features around the coastline of Africa with an attribute rate_time reprepsnting annual rates of change (in metres per year) calculated by linearly regressing annual shoreline distances against time (excluding outliers). Negative values indicate retreat and positive values indicate growth.
column11: AGOL layer: Rate of change of coastlines in Africa
column15: To find working version of data that could be extracted or analysed used AGOL (ArcGIS Online) to search for ‘Digital Earth Africa’ then added layer Rate of change of coastlines in Africa.
column16:
This dataset contain erosion values (meters per year) as points located along coastlines.  Negative values represent erosion while positive values represent coasline increase Processnig steps:

1. Download point shapefile from AGOL for entire continent 
2. Attribute each data point to nearest admin 1  region
3. Exclude non-SADC countries 
4. Compute value per admin1 by averaging all points value

column6: Historic Agricultural Drought Frequency Maps depict the frequency of severe drought in areas where 30 percent/50 percent of the cropland/grassland has been affected. The historical frequency of severe droughts (as defined by ASI) is based on the entire ASI times series (1984-2023).
column15: Added layer from ArcGIS Online.  Filtered to use cropland, with % of land affected > 50%.  Used for both seasons
column16:
1. Data filtered for cropland and area affected > 50%. If data exist for two seasons, the maximum value is used.

2. Value per admin1 region is compute by averaging all pixels within it

3. Data is provided in frequency range (tbc...)

column6: The Global Earthquake Model (GEM) Global Seismic Hazard Map (version 2023.1) depicts the geographic distribution of the Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) with a 10% probability of being exceeded in 50 years, computed for reference rock conditions (shear wave velocity, Vs30, of 760-800 m/s)
column15:
From https://www.globalquakemodel.org/product/global-seismic-hazard-map used Open Version Download
Note: no data for Sechelles or Mauritius
column16:
1. Used zonal statistics to compute mean risk value for a given admin1 region
2. This will be improved to account for population exposure (instead of area exposure - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peak_ground_acceleration)

column6: Deforestation derived from global forest watch data. Input data is derived from Landsat (approx 30m resolution). Tree cover is defined as cannopy closure for all vegetation taller than 5m.  For detail of input datasets see https://data.globalforestwatch.org/documents/941f17325a494ed78c4817f9bb20f33a/explore
column11:
File name format repeated for each tile covering SADC region:
Hansen_GFC-2022-v1.10_treecover2000_20S_020E.tif
Hansen_GFC-2022-v1.10_gain_20S_020E.tif
Hansen_GFC-2022-v1.10_lossyear_20S_020E.tif
column15:
Downloaded all relevant tiles for the three varibales:
- treecover2000
- gain
- lossyear
column16:
1. Merged tiles into a single raster per variable
2. Reclassified treecover (reclass 0 = 0, 1-100 = 1)
3. Reclassified Loss year: reclass 0 = 0, 1-22 = 1  [22 being max year at date of processing
4. Calculated net loss (factoring in gain). Raster calculator used to compute gain - loss.  Remaining values indicate: 0 = no change; 1 =  gain; -1 = loss
5. Summed values for tree cover (2000) and net loss per admin area
6. In table calculated %gain and % loss based on values above

column6: Soil erosion defined as the difference between natural soil erosion and actual soil erosion globally.
column15: Submitted data request via Land degradation debt - ESDAC - European Commission
column16: Used zonal statistics to compute the mean erosion per admin1

column6: Data set representing inundation along the river network, for seven different flood return periods (from 1-in-10-years to 1-in-500-years). 1 in 100 year was chosen for analysis. Input data values provide flood depth in metres.  This was simplified to include any depth of flooding, from which we calaculated the % of the population exposed to a 1 in 100 year flood event
column11: Original tiles in name format: ID127_S21_E10_RP100_depth.tif
column15: Downloaded relevant tiles from https://jeodpp.jrc.ec.europa.eu/ftp/jrc-opendata/CEMS-GLOFAS/flood_hazard/RP100/
column16:
1. Merged together all tiles into a single raster layer
2. Reclassified values to simply represent flood & not flood (value is flood depth. We take all depths in account)
3. Multiplied the population (GHSL 100m) by flood data (ensuring retain resolution of population data)
4. Used Zonal statistics as table to sum the tot population and flooded population per admin area, then calculate the percentage

column6: Qualitative representation of global landslide hazard at a global scale.  Dataset used combines the median annual rainfall triggered landslide hazard with earthquake hazard triggerd, simplifying it into 4 ranks from very low to high
column15: Downloaded the Global Landslide Hazard map (TH ranks) - COG layer as tiff https://datacatalogfiles.worldbank.org/ddh-published/0037584/DR0045417/LS_TH_COG.tif?versionId=2023-01-18T20:43:29.3801128Z
column16:
1. Reclassified two variations of the layer 
High risk (value of 4 = 1, all other values = 0)
Medium rish (value of 3 or 4 = 1, all other values =0)

2. Multiplied reclassified layer by GHSL 2025 population (keeping resolution of population layer) to define population at risk

3. Used zonal statistics as table to compute
- population per admin 1
- population in high landslide risk zones
- population in medium or high landslide risk zones

4. Calculated percentage at risk

column6: Datasets containing tropical cyclone maximum wind speed (in m/s) return periods.  For full description see https://data.4tu.nl/datasets/0ea98bdd-5772-4da8-ae97-99735e891aff/4
column15:
Dowload fixed return period data https://data.4tu.nl/file/0ea98bdd-5772-4da8-ae97-99735e891aff/0b98c6f5-c7af-45bb-bb6e-dac53e1b8d55
Choose layer with the appropriate spatial extent with 100 year return period
column16:
1. Reclassify values (0-32 = 0; x > 33 = 1)
2. Multiply population raster by reclassified layer (keeping resolution of the population data)
3. Use zonal statistics to calculate the population exposed to cyclones per 100 year and compare with baseline population to calculate % exposed

column15: Added layer from AGOL

column6: Global dataset of individual fire perimeters for 2002-2023, provided as polygon features.
column11:
Original layers per year named in format: 
original_globfire_filtered_2023.shp
column15: Downloaded GlobalFire Fire Perimeters [2002 - 2023] from https://gwis.jrc.ec.europa.eu/apps/country.profile/downloads
column16:
1. Merged layers for most recent 5 years
2.Dissolved features to simplify the polygons and added an attribute field containing the value wildfire
3. Ran Union tool to union burnt areas with admin
4. Added new field to attribute table and used Calculate Geometry to calculate the area in km2
5. In the field containing the value wildfire selected all blank entries and assigned a value of ‘none’
6. Created Pivot table to have a row per admin pcode and fields for wildfire & none with values representing area  (km2)
7. Calculated % area burnt [remember to add values for none and burnt to get total area]

column6: Computed from five components. The first three components are numeric ratings of the moisture content of litter  and other fine fuels, the average moisture content of loosely compacted organic layers of moderate depth, and the average moisture content of deep, compact organic layers. The last two components are the rate of fire spread if fuel is available for combustion, and the frontal fire intensity.
column15: Data provided via Tom Hughes as per Madagascar model as data links broken
column16:
1. Used zonal statistics to calculate min, mean, max per admin 1
2. As resolution was course a number of smaller admin areas returned no value. For these centroids were created and Extract Multiple Values to Points tool (in ArcGIS) used to extract value from underlying pixel.

column6: The HIIK's annual publication Conflict Barometer describes the recent trends in global conflict developments, escalations, de-escalations, and settlements.
column7: Heidelberg Institute for International Conflict Research
column15: Download the most recent Map Kit under "Data & Maps" => "Static Maps". After downloading the data and unzipping the folder, the dataset is found in subfolder "HIIK_maps 2022_base data"

column6: The Global Conflict Risk Index (GCRI) estimates the risk of internal conflict at the country level within the next 4 years. INFORM Risk uses the GCRI probability estimates for the “any conflict” category, which includes the sub-categories “state-based conflict”, “non-state conflict”, and “one-sided violence”. The values used on the SADC regional model were obtained directly from the INFORM Global Risk Index
column16: Indicator value on tab "Indicator Data", column "Projected Conflict Probability"

column6: ACLED (Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project) is the most comprehensive public collection of political violence data for developing states. This dataset contains information on the specific dates and locations of political violence, the types of event, the groups involved, fatalities and changes in territorial control. Information is recorded on the battles, killings, riots, and recruitment activities of rebels, governments, militias, armed groups, protesters and civilians. This indicators corresponds to the total number of events over the last 5 years, regardless of fatalities. All types of events are considered.
column15: An account is needed to download ACLED data. After the account is created, a key is generated that allows for downloading data using the provided URL.
column16: All events (regardless of fatalities number) between January 2020 and June 2024. Before the model publication in 2025, this should be recomputed to all 2024 events.

column6: The Human Development Index measure development by combining indicators of life expectancy, educational attainment and income into a composite index.
column15: The creation of an account (free) is needed to download the data. All tables can be downloaded on https://globaldatalab.org/mygdl/downloads/ . This indicator is under "Subnational HDI" -> "Data file"
column16:
The value for this indicator is under column "shdi". 

The subnational subdivision available on the Global Data Lab (GDL) datasets are very similar to the one used here but some differences exist. This is mainly because the GDL keeps the same subdivision over the years for comparison purposes. As a general rule, when one region in the GDL data corresponds to two or more current ones (oftern because of region division over time), we apply the same value to all of them - details on each country is given below. Some subdivisions have a new name or have a different spelling between in the GDL - these cases were manually matched.

Botswana - Data is available for each of the 10 district but not for the 7 cities and towns at admin1 level.

DRC - Data is available following the pre-2015 province division (11 instead of the current 26).

Malawi - Data is available at the district level (admin2) instead of the region level (admin1). This only happens for Malawi and the region value was computed by averaging district values.

Mauritius - No data is available for North and Saint Brandon Islands. For Mauritius Island, its 9 districts are separated into "North" and "South". Rodrigues Island has one value for itself. 

Namibia - Data is available following the pre-2013 region division (13 instead of the current 14).

Seychelles - Data is not available

Tanzania - Data is available following the pre-2002 region division (25 instead of the current 31).

Zambia - Data is available following the pre-2012 province division (9 instead of the current 10).

column6: The GDI measures gender gaps in achievements in three basic dimensions of human development: health (measured by female and male life expectancy at birth), knowledge (measured by female and male expected years of schooling for children and mean years of schooling for adults aged 25 years and older) and living standards (measured by female and male estimated GNI per capita). It is a ratio of the female to the male HDI.

column6: The Multidimensional Poverty MPI Index identifies overlapping deprivations at the household level across the same three dimensions as the Human Development Index (living standards, health, and education) and shows the average number of poor people and deprivations with which poor households contend.
column15: Download available at subnational-level under "Data Table 5: Subnational Results 2023" for most countries. Country-level data used for South Africa, Seychelles and Lesotho ("Data Table 1: National results 2023"). No data for Mauritius
column16:
The subnational subdivision available on the OPHI Poverty dataset is very similar to the one used here but some differences exist. As a general rule, when one region in the OPHI data corresponds to two or more current ones, we apply the same value to all of them. On the other hand, when the OPHI data is more granular, we aggregate values with a population-weighed average. Details on each country is given below.

Botswana - Data at sub-district (admin2) level aggregated at region (admin1) level

Lesotho - Data available at geography regions, not administrative. Using country-level data instead

Madagascar - Antananarivo and Analamanga have different values on the source. Aggregated both for Analamanga

Malawi - Data at district (admin2) level aggregated at region (admin1) level

Mauritius - No data available (national or subnational)

Namibia - Source has a single value for Kavango (instead of Kavango East and Kavango West). Used same value for both

Seychelles - No Subnational-level data available. Using country-level value instead.

South Africa - No Subnational-level data available. Using country-level value instead.

Tanzania - Data available per zone (9 in total) instead of  the 31 regions. Same value used for all regions within the same zone

column6: Net official development assistance (ODA) consists of disbursements of loans made on concessional terms (net of repayments of principal) and grants by official agencies of the members of the Development Assistance Committee (DAC), by multilateral institutions, and by non-DAC countries to promote economic development and welfare in countries and territories in the DAC list of ODA recipients. It includes loans with a grant element of at least 25 percent (calculated at a rate of discount of 10 percent).
column15: Download button (csv, xml, excel) on the main page. Indicator is named "Net ODA received (% of GNI)"
column16: 5-year average (2018-2022). Seychelles most recent data is from 2017 so using a 5-year average between 2013-2017 for this country

column6: Personal transfers consist of all current transfers in cash or in kind made or received by resident households to or from nonresident households. Personal transfers thus include all current transfers between resident and nonresident individuals. Compensation of employees refers to the income of border, seasonal, and other short-term workers who are employed in an economy where they are not resident and of residents employed by nonresident entities. Data are the sum of two items defined in the sixth edition of the IMF's Balance of Payments Manual: personal transfers and compensation of employees. Data are in current U.S. dollars.
column15: Download button (csv, xml, excel) on the main page. Indicator is named "Personal remittances, received (% of GDP)"
column16: 5-year average (2019-2023)

column6: Differentiation between different levels of severity of acute food insecurity, classifying units of analysis in five distinct phases: (1) Minimal/None, (2) Stressed, (3) Crisis, (4) Emergency, (5) Catastrophe/Famine.

column6: Density of health facilities per capita. The HDX database of health facilities was developed from a variety of government and non-government sources from 50 countries in sub-Saharan Africa. column16: The total number of health facilities per admin1 region is computed through a geospatial join between the facilities' coordinates and the administrative boundaries. During calibration, this value is divided by the admin1 population to obtain a density indicator

column5: People using at least basic sanitation services (% of population)
column6: The percentage of people using at least basic sanitation services, that is, improved sanitation facilities that are not shared with other households.  This indicator encompasses both people using basic sanitation services as well as those using safely managed sanitation services.   Improved sanitation facilities include flush/pour flush to piped sewer systems, septic tanks or pit latrines; ventilated improved pit latrines, compositing toilets or pit latrines with slabs.
column7: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply and Sanitation
column15: Data download directly from the download's page (https://washdata.org/data/downloads) under "Household" => "World file"
column16: Data available from 2022 for all countries except Comoros (2019). Tab "Sanitation", column "Total" => "At least basic"

column5: People using at least basic drinking water services (% of population)
column6: The percentage of people using at least basic water services.  This indicator encompasses both people using basic water services as well as those using safely managed water services.  Basic drinking water services is defined as drinking water from an improved source, provided collection time is not more than 30 minutes for a round trip.  Improved water sources include piped water, boreholes or tubewells, protected dug wells, protected springs, and packaged or delivered water.

column6:
Mean years schooling of population 25+. The mean years of schooling 
of adults aged 25+ (MYS), reflects the current situation with regard to 
education in a society.
Mean years of schooling was computed by using the Global Data Lab 
Area Database, taking for each region the average number of years of 
education completed by adults aged 25 and over in the survey and census 
datasets. In most of these datasets educational level is measured in 
years of education completed. In a restricted number of cases, education
 was measured by the highest completed education level. In those cases, 
the data was turned into years of education on the basis of information 
on the number of years it normally takes to complete a certain level in 
the specific country.

column6:
The Subnational Corruption Database (SCD) provides data on corruption
 in 1,473 subnational areas of 178 countries. The SCD includes an 
overall corruption index, the Subnational Corruption Index (SCI), and 
its two components: the Subnational Grand Corruption Index (SGCI) and 
Subnational Petty Corruption Index (SPCI). The SCD is constructed by 
combining data of 807 surveys held in the period 1995–2022 and includes 
the corruption experiences and perceptions of 1,326,656 respondents 
along 19 separate dimensions. The data are available for multiple years,
 allowing longitudinal analyses. At the national level, the SCI 
correlates strongly with established corruption indices, like the 
Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI) and the 
World Bank Control of Corruption Index (CCI).
The Comprehensive SCI uses methods of 
estimation, interpolation and extrapolation to arrive at a full series 
for each area. These comprehensive data are most appropriate for 
descriptive purposes or within-year comparisons.
The SCD makes use of 13 different sources which each offer 
subnationally classified corruption data on at least one of the 19 
corruption dimensions. The SCD harmonizes all subnational 
classifications into one coherent and consistent classification.
